title: Sara Blakely of SPANX Speaks at The Edge Connection ...
text:
[Music]
Thank you very much, Edge Connection,
for having me and thank you to all of
you for getting up extra early today.
I'm going to share my personal journey
to success with you, but I have to throw
a disclaimer in there because my journey
includes the words Spanx, crotch, and
g-string. So, for the men in the room,
brace yourselves.
About 15 years ago, I remember I was
sitting in a convention. I was a fax
sales rep and with the company I worked
for, Dena, I was there because I was one
of the top sales reps in the country.
And at that point in my life, I had
always wanted to be a lawyer, but I
basically failed the LSAT. I'm not a
good test taker. So, I found myself I
after that driving to Disney World and
trying out to be goofy with my college
degree, making my family very, very
proud. And unfortunately, you have to be
5'8 to be goofy. And I'm 5'6, so they
made me a chipmunk.
And that didn't work out. So there I was
selling fax machines doortodoor sitting
at this convention and this larger
than-l life speaker got up on stage and
he said I'm going to prove to you today
in four words that there is no such
thing as a bad idea and he continued on
to go teenage mutant ninja turtle.
So, I never in my wildest dreams could
have imagined sitting there that five
years later I would come up with an
invention and be able to prove to the
world in two words that there was no
such thing as a bad idea and that is
footless panty hose. It it all started
with my own butt. I did not like the way
that it looked in white pants. So, just
like so many women out there, we
struggle on what to wear under things.
There are traditional shapers that were
too thick and left lines and bulges.
There was underwear that leaves a panty
line. And then along came the thong,
which still completely confuses me
because all that did was put underwear
exactly where we had been trying to get
it out of.
So, I decided to take matters into my
own hands and I cut the feet out of
control top panty hoes, threw them on
under my white pants. Looked fabulous,
felt great, but they rolled up my legs
all night. And at the end of that night,
I went to a party in Buckhead. I said to
myself, I have got to figure out a way
to create this so that it stays down on
the leg. I didn't know what to do. I
like I had mentioned, I'd never taken a
business class before. I was working
selling fax machines during the day. I
ended up getting on the internet and
looking up hosery mills and I went to a
website called thomasnet.com
where I found a list of all US
manufacturers and I literally went down
the list finding hosery and shapewear
and started calling each one of them on
the phone. Well, I did this for about
six to eight months and everybody said
the same thing which was basically no. I
believed in my product. I still wanted
to get it made. So, I started going to
all the local craft stores here in
Atlanta. I went to Michaels. I went to
Hancock Fabric and I started making
homemade versions of my idea, buying
little stretchy elastic lace, paper
clipping them, sewing them to the ends
of the panty hose. I needed it to stop
just below the knee because that way it
was invisible on your thigh and you got
all the great shaping under your pants.
At the same time that I'm trying to get
this made at the manufacturers, I
decide, you know what? I believe in this
idea. I want to patent it. So, I ended
up calling. I went on the internet and I
looked up at Martin Hubble three A-rated
law firms here in Atlanta. I called them
on the phone. I said, "I'd like to make
an appointment." I took my lucky red
backpack and I went to each one of the
calls. And the first one that I went to
later admitted to me that he thought my
idea was so bad that he thought I had
been sent by candid camera.
So, you can imagine I'm presenting to
him my footless panty hose and telling
him how I'm going to change the world,
one woman at a time. And he is looking
around the room during my entire
presentation, apparently for the hidden
camera. And they all quoted me between
three and $5,000 to patent my idea. And
I had $5,000 total in my savings to do
this idea. So, I decided to write my own
patent. I went to Barnes & Noble on
Peach Tree Drive and I bought a book
called Patents and Trademarks and I
started writing my own patent. At the
same time that I'm writing it, I'm still
determined to get it made. So, I take a
week off of work and drive around North
Carolina and go in person to all the
manufacturers that had been telling me
no thanks on the phone. So, there I
went. I got my lucky red backpack, drove
up to North Carolina, went from
manufacturer to manufacturer, and I
would hear the same three questions
every time, which was, "And you are?"
And I would say, "Sarah Blakeley." And
they'd say, "And you're with." And I'd
say, "Sarah Blakeley."
And they go, "And you're financially
backed by." And I'd go, "Sarah
Blakeley."
And they go, "So nice to meet you. Good
luck with your idea." and walk me to the
door. And it was about 2 to 3 weeks
after I did that road show where
everyone told me no again in person that
I got a phone call from a manufacturer
in Charlotte, North Carolina. And he
said, quote, "Sarah, I have decided to
help make your crazy idea." And when I
asked him why he had the change of
heart, he simply said, "I have two
daughters."
Lucky for me, he ran it by his
daughters. They said, "Dad, it's a
brilliant idea. you got to help this
girl make it. And that set me on my way
of trying to get it made. Now, as a
consumer, it had never dawned on me that
men were the ones making all of our
undergarments. But it kind of dawned on
me that maybe that's why they've been so
uncomfortable for so long because the
the people making them were not spending
all day in them. And if they were, they
certainly weren't admitting it. So,
[Laughter]
it caused some challenges in the
beginning because everyone that I was
talking to was a man and I'm trying to
explain. No, it's not just about being
footless. It's about right here. It's
making things look better and smoother
and no panty lines. Well, through the
process, I learned three very
interesting things that stood out to me
as a consumer. One was the industry had
been taking an average waistband size
between a small and an extra large and
putting that same waistband size on
every single product in order to cut
cost in production. So I immediately
said, "Nope, that does not make sense.
That's why we're cutting our waistbands.
That's why we can't breathe." So with
Spanx, if you're a small, you get a
small waistband. If you're a large, you
get a large waistband. It fits and makes
the garment stay and work with the
woman's body much better. The second
thing was the way that they did the
sizing was very interesting to me. They
have plastic forms and they would say
this is plastic form small. This is
plastic form medium and large and etc.
And they would make the garment and then
put it on the plastic form and they
would all stand back with their
clipboards and go, "Yep, that's a
medium."
And I would nudge them and say, "Ask her
how she feels."
So, I remember very quickly, I'm going
to test all of my Spanx products on
women. I test them on my mom, my
grandmother, my friends, and I have them
wear the products. And we still do this.
We have a lot of women that work at
Spanx. They wear the products. The men
at Spanx, their wives wear it. And now
the men at Spanx also will wear the
products. And they're very honest. We
get feedback over time. We don't just
put it on a form and say it fit, it
stays. And the last thing was just
overall the yarns and the way that the
machines were working. I found that it,
you know, really took a lot of technical
time to make the fit and the product
stay and and you want more compression
in certain areas and not so much in the
other. And the industry had kind of just
been making things all one compression
which didn't necessarily work as well
with the woman's body. So I took all
this in. I'm still trying to get my
product patented. I call up the lawyer
that laughed and thought I was on candid
camera. I said, 'Ive basically made the
garment. Will you please, please help me
write the claims? I've written my entire
patent. I had my mom, who's an artist,
do the sketch that's in the patent
that's still on the US patent and
trademark offic's um website. And she
she actually sketched it in our living
room at home. I went home for a weekend
and I was the model. I stood there in
the product and that's what we did for
that. So, I had done basically
everything I could except for the
claims, which is when you have the real
legal need to write the claims portion
of a patent. I ended up calling him. He
said, "Sarah, I still don't get this
idea, but you're so passionate about it
for $700. I'll I'll do it over a
weekend." I said, "Great." So, he spent
the weekend doing it. And on that
Monday, he called me and he said, "I
just need to speak to the person at the
manufacturer that you've been closely
working with so I can find out exactly
what the technical aspects are of this
garment." I said, "No problem. That's
Ted." So Ted and I had been spending a
lot of time working together for the
past year. I'd been driving up there at
night and on the weekend trying to get
the prototype just right. Well, I got to
tell you, Ted was very southern. I mean,
when I say southern, I mean real
southern. So, we get Ted on the phone. I
introduce. We're doing a three-way call.
I said, "Ted, this is my attorney. Can
you please tell us exactly what's in the
Spanx garment?" And he said, "Yeah,
they're 70% nylon and 30% lacquer."
Okay, thanks, Ted. So, my attorney and I
are taking notes furiously. I I thank
everybody. We hang up the phone and the
night before I'm going to submit my
patent to the US patent and trademark
office. I can't sleep. I remember
sitting up thinking, "How does this
product have lacquer in it?"
So the next morning I called Ted and I
said, "Ted, can you please spell
lacquer?" And he said, "Sure.
L Y C R A.
Oh my god. Lyra.
Okay. Thank you, Ted.
So, I called my attorney. I said, "Get
on your computer and hit all change on
lacquer. It's Lycra." And he started
laughing so hard. He goes, "Sarah, do
you know how fast you would have gotten
a patent trying to make pantyhose out of
paint thinner?"
So, I went online and I trademarked the
word Spanx uh at uspto.gov
and it cost me $300 on my credit card.
And then I also incorporated online at
incorporate.com and I put my credit card
in and that was $150.
And the word Spanx is interesting. It
came to me after thinking of really bad
names for about a year. I knew I wanted
my product to have a fabulous name. I
just didn't know what it was. And
sitting in traffic in Atlanta. I
remember the word Spanx came to me
across the windshield and I literally
pulled off the side of the road, wrote
it down on a piece of paper and I knew
it was it. I went home that night and I
actually got online and trademarked it.
At the very last minute, I changed the
KS to an X because I had done research
and knew that madeup words do better for
products than real words do. And they're
also a lot easier to trademark. Now, how
did the word Spanx come to me? A lot of
people ask. After many, many bad names
running through my head. One day, I
decided, okay, Kodak and CocaCola are
two of the most recognized names in the
world. What do they have in common? They
both have a strong K sound to them. And
someone told me that the man who came up
with the name Kodak, it's a madeup word
and he actually took the K because he
liked the sound of it and put it at the
beginning and the end of the word and
played with letters in the alphabet. I
also have friends that do standup comedy
and it's a weird trade secret among
comedians that the K sound will make
your audience laugh. So, I put all that
together and I decided the K sound was
good luck. And the minute I thought
that, the word Spanx came to me and I
knew it was right. As far as the
packaging goes, what was I going to do?
I knew I needed to do something
different. I am a consumer. Everyone is
a consumer of an idea they come up with.
You know what you like and don't like in
the space that you've been buying. And
for me, I go into the hosery shapewear
department and it's a sea of beige and
gray. Every package looked the same. It
was the same half- naked woman that had
been photographed on every package for
the last 20 years. And you had to read
the fine print in the upper lefthand
corner to figure out what was going on.
So I knew that I wanted my package to be
bold, be different. If I got that chance
for shelf space, it needed to stand out
and say, "I'm new. I'm different. Check
me out." I had no money to advertise. So
I chose red, which was extremely
unconventional. No one had ever done red
in the hosery or shapewear space. And I
did something no one had ever done
either. I put three cartoon illustrated
looking women on the cover. And the
three women were very different looking.
And no one had ever done that either. It
had always been photography on the front
of the package. I worked on this package
at night and on the weekends at my
friend's computer for about three
months. And when I got it just right,
just how I wanted it to be, and I didn't
have any guidance. I just thought I want
it to be something pretty that I would
want to buy. I kind of want this to feel
like a present to myself instead of a
commodity I'm dreading to buy. I ended
up taking it, putting it down in my
apartment and saying, "It's perfect, but
what if there is supposed to be
something on here for legal reasons that
I don't know about?" So, I went to
Bloomingdales and a few different stores
and I bought 10 different pairs of panty
hoes. And I came back to my apartment
and I put every single pair on the floor
and I remember looking and if the same
thing was on all 10, I remember thinking
it must be legal.
So I added it.
Then once I had my packaging, the
prototype, I was ready to go. I had my
name. I called Neiman Marcus.
And the Neiman Marcus here in Atlanta
kind of laughed and said, "Well, Sarah,
there is a buying office and it's in
Dallas, so why don't you call that
number?" So, I called the Dallas Nean
Marcus and asked for the buyer and said,
"I'm Sarah Blakeley. I invented a
product that's going to change the way
women wear clothes. If you give me 10
minutes of your time, I will fly there
and show you." And she said, "If you're
willing to fly here, I will give you 10
minutes." I jumped on a plane and
despite the pleas of my friends, I took
my lucky red backpack from college. My
friends said, "Please, Sarah, you cannot
go to the Neiman Marcus headquarters
with that old red backpack. Anything.
Buy a Prada bag. Return it the next day
if you need to do something."
But I took a really deep breath and I
remember thinking, "This is my lucky red
backpack. I have to bring it." So I
jumped on the plane and off I went. And
I'm sitting in front of the most
impeccably dressed woman. I mean, even
her pen was fabulous. And I was shaking.
I had my footless Spanx in a Ziploc bag
from my kitchen, and I had a color copy
of the packaging I had created on my
friend's computer.
Halfway through my presentation, about
five minutes into it, I don't know if it
was nerves or just sh no shame or this
was my shot. I just took a deep breath
and said, "You know what? You need to
come to the bathroom with me." She kind
of went, "Excuse me?" I said, "Just
please come to the bathroom with you. I
with me. I'm going to show you what my
product can do. And she followed me into
the bathroom and sure enough I did my
own before and after for her and
instantly she got it. And instantly she
said, "Sarah, this is brilliant. I'm
going to try it in seven of our stores."
I was beyond excited. I got in the
rental car and on my way to the airport,
the first call I made was to the
manufacturer and the mill owner in
Charlotte, North Carolina. And I said,
"Sam, Sam, it's Sarah. I need more. He
said, "Excuse me?" I said, "I need more
Spanx. I just got off with Neiman Marcus
and they want to order it and we have to
ship it to seven stores." Dead silence.
I said, "Sam, are you there?" He goes,
"Sarah, I I had to I have to admit, I
thought you were going to give these
away as Christmas gifts and presents for
the next five years. What do you mean
Nean Marcus wants it?" I said, "Ne just
placed a big order and I need more." And
he said, "Well, okay. Congratulations.
Let me patch you through to Ted.
So Ted picks up the phone. I said, "Ted,
Ted, it's Sarah. I need more Spanx." And
he said, "Well, that's great, but what
you going to do about the crotches?"
Excuse me. Don't they come with
crotches?
He said, "Well, yeah, but we only got
two crotch machines and they're being
used by somebody else."
Okay, let me get this straight. I just
landed Neiman Marcus and I have no
crotches.
I don't know where to go for a crotch.
I looked in the yellow pages
under crotch.
It's not there.
So then I found out that there's a fancy
word for crotch, which is gusset, which
helped my search. And I was on the phone
and my roommate at the time would laugh
because we would have these FedEx
packages arrive every day from all over
the world. and she'd come home, she was
a teacher, she'd come home and go, "Got
another crotch in the mail." And lucky
for me, the company I ended up using was
right down the road in Norcross,
Georgia. And they to this day provide us
with our gussets. And just to make the
story even that much more unbelievable,
the man that saved the day was an
80-year-old man by the name of Jean
Bobo. Kind of a legend in the business.
He said, "Sarah, I got your crotches for
you. They'll be there in time for the
Neiman Marcus order. And I was able to
ship to the seven stores.
Well, I immediately called every friend
I ever remotely had in those seven
cities and said, "Hi, it's Sarah. Do you
remember me?" We sat next to each other
in fourth grade. Um,
can you go to the store and say you're
looking for something called Spanx and
you've been wanting it your whole life
and I'll send you a check?
Well, just as I was running out of money
and friends, Oprah called. I had sent a
gift basket of my very first batch of
prototypes to the Oprah Winfrey show.
And I get the call that Oprah has chosen
Spanx as her favorite product of the
year.
You can imagine for an entrepreneur with
no money. I'm sitting in my apartment.
That's a call of a lifetime. I was
beyond thrilled. They said, Sarah, in
two weeks, Oprah's going to hold up
Spanx. She doesn't normally have people
on the show because it's all about
product, but she loves what you're
doing. You've you've broke into a two
billion dollar male-dominated industry
with no experience and no money. We want
to show a little clip about what you're
doing. So, we're going to fly down and
film you. I said, "Great." So, they all
showed up at my apartment and there were
about 10 of them. They were so
impressive. Clipboards, video cameras,
big lighting. And they said, "Sarah, on
the plane, we decided that we want to
film you in your headquarters."
I said, "You're here."
They kind of looked at each other and
went, "Oh, okay. Okay. Well, we also
decided on the plane that we want to
film you having a staff meeting."
Hold on a minute. I called Connie that
I'd met at mailboxes, etc.
I said, "Connie, it's Sarah. Can you be
at my apartment in five minutes and look
like you work for me?" She said, "I'm on
my way." And I also called all my
friends who left their jobs and came
over and we sat in a circle on the floor
of my apartment and that was my staff
meeting on the Oprah Winfrey show.
So, one day you wake up and you're an
inventor and then all of a sudden
another day you wake up and you're a
company.
That's a different feeling. I remember
it very well. My only employee at the
time was my boyfriend who was a health
care consultant. He was watching me stay
up all night shipping pantyhos around
the world. He said, "You need help." He
left his job. Then my second employee
was a friend of a friend who heard I
needed an assistant because I couldn't
manage my scheduling and flights and
booking this and going and doing
appearances to try to spread the word
and all that was going on in the
company. So, she came on board and after
two weeks of being my assistant, I said,
"Do you want to be my head of product
development
and she said, "Sure." And she still is.
And the third one was a friend that I
went to walk to get a bagel with and she
said, "I've tried your prototype. It's
amazing. I love it." She told me the
whole walk there and the whole walk back
why she loved it. I said, "Do you want
to be my head of PR?"
And she did. And um about, you know, a
month into the business, Dave and I
mostly, we were at two cardboard tables
at the back room of my apartment. It was
a two-bedroom apartment. And the phone
would always ring and and we would say,
hello, this is Spanx. And they would
say, um, I need to speak to shipping and
handling. We go, please hold while I
transfer you.
And I I literally ran the business out
of my apartment for a year and a half.
And a few months in, of course, what's
the next thing that we decide to do? I
look at my team, my robust team of three
and said, I'm going to Europe. Gotta
bring Spanx overseas. So, I grabbed my
lucky red backpack, jumped on a plane,
cold called Herods, Harvey Nix, and
Selfridges. And I got a chance to be on
the BBC, which is basically
the US CNN. And the gentleman that was
interviewing me, you know, million
people, no pressure, Sarah, he starts
out and goes, "So Sarah, tell us what
Spanx can do for women in the UK."
I said, "Well, it's all about the fanny.
It smooths your fanny. It lifts and
separates your fanny." And this man lost
all the color in his face.
And I had no idea, but apparently fanny
means vagina. in England.
[Applause]
Oops.
Obviously, I had to call back to our
team and say our international expansion
is off to a great start.
And from there, it's Spanx is now 10
years old. We just had our 10-year
anniversary. So much has happened in the
10 years. We had a a company meeting and
I I said at the at the meeting, 10 years
ago, no one had ever heard of an iPod.
People were not using Blackberries. The
Kardashians were in fifth grade.
Spanx went from one to over 100
employees.
We launched from just one footless panty
hose. Now we're in swim. We've created
bras that are amazing. They're called
Bralleluja. If you haven't tried it, I
hope you will. It's become my favorite
product. And men's. We've done so many
things with Spanx. And it has exceeded
truly my wildest dreams. I never could
have imagined all that had come from
this. When I could afford to hire my
weaknesses, I did early on. I have a
fabulous team and a great CEO. She runs
the day-to-day. Anyone who knows and has
started a business has had to be every
department in the beginning and you
learn very quickly not good at this. I
really like this. And when you can
afford to do it, delegate. It's a huge
gift to yourself and to your company's
success. But the thing that I'm most
proud of over the last 10 years is that
Spanx has given over $10 million to help
women more than just their rears. So,
and we've done it in many different ways
and one of them was obviously giving and
wanting to support the Edge Connection.
It's a fabulous organization and in
doing that we've helped women with going
to college with their own
entrepreneurial businesses and the the
thing I like the most about it that's
the most special to me is that every
woman we help gets her very own lucky
red backpack. So it's my opportunity to
pay it forward and thank you so much for
letting me be here today and speak to
you. I think we have a coupon for each
one of you. So if you go to spanx.com
and you have to spell it right or you're
going to get a real treat.
My mom sent her whole lunchon to the
wrong website when I first started out.
So, spa and x and there's $25 off any of
our garments. And a lot of things on
spanx.com are $30. So, you're basically
getting um to try something for free. If
you've never tried it before and you're
a woman, I suggest the power panties,
which is also called the in power line.
It's a little shaper short that will
take you through the whole spring and
summer. And guys, we have not forgotten
about you. We have reinvented and
redesigned the man's undershirt. Our
thought there was not so much shaping,
shaping, shaping, but how can we make it
better? The man's undershirt has been
the exact same for a hundred years.
Nobody's paid any attention to it. It's
boxy and bulky under styles and sweaters
and dress shirts. So, it's more tapered
in. It's more streamline. It's got it's
cotton, so it looks and acts just like
your regular undershirt, but it's got a
little bit of secret power going on
around the the waist area, which is also
giving men a lot of great uh lower back
support. So, check it out. Thanks.com.
Thank you so much for having me here
today, and good luck with your journeys
to success.
[Music]
Thank you. Heat. Heat.
[Music]
